youtube_items.title: [YT] A world going hungry? How conflict and climate change disrupt global food supply | Business Beyond
youtube_items.summary: The world is struggling to feed itself. A planet that once looked like it was winning the  war on starvation is now heading for disaster. “There is one person dying from hunger every  minute across Ethiopia, Kenya and Somalia.   In West Africa, the region is facing  its worst hunger crisis in a decade But when there’s never been more food and there’s  never been more farming, how is this possible? It's an extraordinary situation, isn't it? A world  of great abundance. I mean, we have a massive   surplus of food, twice as many calories are  produced than humans need to support themselves,   and yet we see rising hunger once again Keep watching to find out what brought   us here. youtube_items.title: [YT] FoodSystems 2030: Transforming Food Systems for People, Planet, and Prosperity
youtube_items.summary: Hunger is on the rise with over 295 million people facing severe food insecurity in 2024. Current food systems generate a third of global emissions and drive the loss of forest and biodiversity. Meeting sustainable development goals require shifting towards a more circular economy. So every part of the food system delivers better outcomes from farm to table. To achieve this, the World Bank leads Food Systems 2030, a multi-donor trust fund that is rebuilding food systems. Food systems 2030 targets every stage in the food value chain to ensure a healthier people, planet, [music] and economy. Food Systems 2030 envisions a world where by 2030 people can afford a healthy and sustainable diet and have healthier lives with less illness and disease. This means working across all aspects of food sy

youtube_items.title: [YT] What Earth in 2050 could look like - Shannon Odell
youtube_items.summary: While we’re already feeling the devastating effects of human-caused climate change, governments continue to fall short on making and executing emissions pledges that would help thwart further warming. So, what will our world look like in the next 30 to 80 years, if we continue on the current path? While it’s impossible to know exactly how the next decade will unfold, scientists and climate experts have made projections, factoring in the current state of affairs. This future we’re about to describe is bleak, but remember there’s still time to ensure it doesn’t become our reality. It’s 2050. We’ve blown past the 1.5 degree target that world leaders promised to stick to. The Earth has warmed 2 degrees since the 1800s, when the world first started burning fossil fuels in mass scale. raw_items.summary: 11 July 2025. Global Report on Food Crises (GRFC) 2025. Analyses assessments and case studies Food security analysis (VAM).In 2024, more than 295 million people across 53 countries and territories experienced acute levels of hunger – an increase of 13.7 million from 2023.

raw_items.title: Global Food Insecurity: Trends and Policy... - The Borgen Project
raw_items.summary: Food Insecurity: Global Trends. Global progress toward SDG 2 (Zero Hunger) remains stagnant since 2021. The broader picture reveals that hunger rates have plateaued since spiking in 2019.

raw_items.title: Supply Chain Disruptions May Accelerate... - Responsibleus
raw_items.summary: Rising supply chain risks linked to conflict, climate change and trade disruptions could drive greater investment in regenerative agriculture and sustainable food production, according to Ecovia Intelligence. Responsible Us Bureau Jun 17, 2026 Add to Reading List.

raw_items.title: How Indonesia's Food System Can Weather Supply-Chain Shocks
raw_items.summary: In that same timeframe, reported food shortages rose from 19% to 31.2%. Those trends expose how quickly healthy diets can become unaffordable when supply-chain disruptions reverberate across household incomes, schools, health services, and food supply chains.

raw_items.title: Thanks to climate change, supply chain disruptions are... | Salon.com
raw_items.summary: Like so many other features of capitalism, the development of long supply chains seems poised to self-immolate because of climate change. Unfortunately, there is no sign that the economic titans are interested in changing the way supply chains work at this juncture.
